Good afternoon everybody. Welcome to the first of your three calls with Dan Kennedy that are part of your participation in the business of copywriting Academy that you'll be you'll be attending in May. We're thrilled to have you with us and we're anxious to get get get hearing from Dan. Today we're going to discuss the five things clients want to know but may not ask. When we go through the after we're done going through those we're going to Dan's going to give us a few comments about preparing for the Academy and then there'll be an opportunity for some Q&A. The group is muted until then till that point when we have the Q&A then one at a time you'll be invited to ask a question. So Dan let's get started. What are these five questions all about? Well, they're what I call the keys to trust so they're all about creating trust with with with the client and and I think it's important to understand that that's that's one of the most important things that we sell. I mean we deliver copy but we sell a number of things other than copy. One of them is trust and it influences compensation and it influences the cooperation or the compliance that we get from the client. You know, I always think I mean this is my modus operandi hasn't changed appreciably in more than 20 years. So now, you know, what motivates the first-time client to get on a plane and travel to Cleveland, especially this time of year, schlep about an hour from the airport by car, stay overnight in the hotel he's told to stay in and be waiting for me at 8.30 in the morning and pay $18,800 to spend the day with me and then retain me to write copy. Well, yeah, there's reputation. Maybe there's some celebrity but the main reasons that this occurs aren't just available to me and they aren't just available after great tenure. Again, my modus operandi hasn't changed in 20 plus years and they are replicatable and these five questions are really an important part of being able to exercise that kind of control over the client. Well, let's start with question number one. All right, they're not, I wouldn't put them necessarily in any order, but and more importantly, these are not necessarily questions that if given the opportunity the client would ask or really even know to ask, but they're underlying everything that's going on. So the first one is do you have a scientific process for achieving success? And so really what a client wants to know is that there's some regimen, there's some system, there's some logic to what it is that you do, that essentially you're not winging it, not unique to the copy business. We try and engineer it into every marketing scenario that I've ever been involved in as a as an odd example, maybe, but a number of years ago for about five years a client of mine was a major marketing advisor to the carpet cleaning industry and and one of the things that we invented for all of the carpet cleaners was what we called a carpet audit. And so the carpet cleaning technician arrives at your home and before the X number of dollars a square foot and GD you want to add stainless move a kind of typical conversation there's this whole diagnostic process to going room by room putting little different colored flags where different kinds of stains are mapping the whole thing out and so there's a process in chiropractic where I work for many years chiropractors call it report of findings so there's a diagnostic process and then there's a presentation called a report of findings which basically shows you how the diagnosis controls the treatment and so confidence comes from seeing an organized approach and when prescription pre-seeds diagnostics trust waivers so sort of the the menu price approach to our business hey, I want I want a four-page letter in an envelope with a buck slip and an order form and how much do you charge to do that? that's prescription ahead of diagnostics and and really the opposite should occur the patient shouldn't be prescribing The client should not be ordering off a menu He really wants to know that there's a more sophisticated process in play than that And then so Dan is is there going to be some kind of audit system or or how are you going to then? teach us When we're there how to do this well, I'll show you at the academy several different approaches to this Some some consultants use a very very formulaic questionnaire based approach to this Others use a more conversational approach but what I will show you is how to get off of the Client ordering from menu and priceless approach to the business so that a more complex Conversation occurs it immediately turns the tables. I mean that's very powerful. That's exciting. Yeah through that conversation A demonstration occurs right that there's a that there's that there's a real science to this There's a real process to this. It's not magic. It's not voodoo or yeah, and it's not art and it's not Maybe the worst thing it could be is it's not winging it right right well, I will throw it against the wall and see what sticks kind of thing Well, that's that's great. What's number two? This links to the first one and it's almost always neglected the question really is do you have a governing philosophy and If you think about Again the healthcare analogy So if you're going to see a new medical doctor a new primary physician You may want somebody who is By the book I May want somebody whose philosophy is The classic first do no harm and second we try everything Natural before we resort to prescription drugs Somebody else may want someone who is even more they may want a natural path They may want a fully holistic practice practitioner and on and on and so If we if we sort of get a referral from a friend or we interview docs What we're looking for whether we're able to enunciate it or not is what is the governing philosophy? is that all Decisions and recommendations now Are controlled by and our clients are really Looking for the same thing When I began to teach myself Advertising and maybe more importantly the advertising business I studied Ogilvy first and then others kind of of the era wondermen we use as well as contemporaries as like sugarman halberd and What what what I recognized is is that everyone every one of them not only had a Philosophy about advertising but they enunciated So that clients clearly understood What they were about To use colloquial language where they were coming from so there was a foundation for recommendations and methodology mine a simplified version of mine is Enunciated in the first three chapters of my book no BS direct marketing Which is like required reading by the way for a brand new client who might surface and So that's my explanation of Here's how I approach getting results for you and here's why and And if that's absent if that's missing if that question is never answered Then your methodology your recommendations and your deliverables are Constantly questioned much more than when the philosophy is enunciated and understood So when you are able to put that in place You have a lot less problems with clients They have more reason to have confidence and trust in you Because you've got some sort of navigational system in place for what you're doing and why you are doing it the way you are doing That's great. That's great. Now. What is number three on your list number three is pretty cool because Anybody can succeed at answering this question Whether they hung out their shingle as a copywriter last week Or they've been at it for the last 30 years. The question is do you do your homework? Nobody really wants to Get up to get an airplane and get up in the air with a spontaneous pilot. I know I Think everybody would agree with that you know, I fly private and I get a little nervous if I see the guy sticking the garment on the dashboard with the suction cup You know when we're taking off I kind of hope that he's like squatted the whole trip and Check the weather and so forth and so Really what clients want to know is that? You don't just make stuff up That you have that you do research that you gather information and The minute you demonstrate that by the way you gain an enormous Amount of trust so so there's several things that you that you want to do in this in this category With this question number one is if you can you want to evidence? depth of background so Over time of course smart copywriters all tend to niche so I do I've done a fair amount of health weight loss and nutrition for example, so I can point to examples and converse about Company after company after company case history after case history But early on in those niches I At least made myself able to converse about company after company and case history after case history Even though I had nothing to do with it But I had done my homework and so When I got my first major client for example in the weight loss industry I was able from the very beginning to intelligently talk about how their competitors had built their business How they had built their business? What some of the sacred cows of marketing weight loss Were So I had some depth of background that I could evidence Secondly you want to demonstrate? Investigation before the first meeting if at all possible and of course the internet has made this easier than ever before Because pretty much you can Google anybody and anything and something's coming up So it's just inexcusable to go into a meeting or a telephone conversation Or even correspondence with a potential client without having done a little bit of background work and Know something about that company about that person and about the industry that they are in I Hardly ever now dealing with new clients in my case who I'm sort of predisposed right But even with reoccurring clients, so I have a day coming up on Monday with a client that often on For more than a decade I've done work for and it's been sometimes since I've done anything of significance for them And this particular aspect of their business is not something I've had anything to do with In five or six years, so not only did I get a big prep package of stuff from them before the meeting But I Went we got the appropriate trade journals for the past year I had somebody pull stuff out of DM news direct marketing target, etc About what their competitors are doing What's going on in this particular product category in this industry? So I've done several hours of Of homework Before I'll walk in the room with these folks and And I will early on Not explain that but I will demonstrate that sure so they will feel like I'm you know in touch and And it's got to have the an effect on you too as the as the copywriter's got to give you confidence as well too Well, yeah, you don't Ideally you don't want to be surprised right and so a Certain amount of knowledge is like insurance against looking stupid But beyond that They instantly feel better when they realize sure taken this seriously And you've prepared and then last and this is really important In this category you want to present what I call an information gathering path That is good occur before copy gets written so Trust from the client will be much higher if there is a Process of collecting and analyzing information between Getting hired and running home to the computer to write copy And again, we'll talk about it the Academy some people are very very formal about this. I used to be a decade ago And so the client literally gets you know a questionnaire and a homework list and so forth others are a little more in their head about it and it varies client by client but Options here include for example that you're going to interview their top salespeople Or you're actually good to focus groups and customers or you're going to go to a Place where all the customers gather So two years ago. I did one of my biggest projects ever for a big retail healthcare company more than a thousand outlets across the country and They really came to me to get advertising. They wound up getting a lot more, but that's what they came for and this information gathering path That I laid out included interviewing Some of the top franchisees by phone Without them present by the way interviewing top salespeople by phone getting transcripts of salespeople wired up delivering their presentations Talking to customers By phone Running my own little focus group Mystery shopping competitors Mystery shopping a couple of their offices all preparatory to really doing Any copywriting and from their standpoint a whole list of things they needed to do like gather up everything That's been done before with as much information about results has as they had and Often this leads you to things they will never tell you That when you know them and feed them feed it back to them in a certain way again Gains you some power and control across as well as Making it possible for you to do your job better in in their case for example Through this process. I discovered three critically important things one that the primary motivations for their customers purchases were dramatically different than the way they ranked them To that there was a timing sweet spot for the buyer That was event driven and was controllable in the selection and use of mailing lists that They knew but hadn't used or attributed any importance to and Third that there was a celebrity a personality to endorse the product that they already owned They weren't attaching any importance to if I hadn't done the digging none of those three things would have come to me But the very fact of doing the digging Builds a lot of confidence in a client sure And in this case this was a million dollar project for a client who? Was not like a Dan Kennedy fan for a long period of time So so trust was pretty important That's fabulous. That's that's really exciting Talking about people not knowing Doing any investigation beforehand. I think it's happened twice in our 13 years that Somebody has approached me More on the marketing side Think you know and and have asked questions like you know, where are the lawyers? You know thinking that we're copyright Righ ght. Yep, so Okay, so let's just restate the question so far number one that we discussed this evening Do you have a process and we covered that number two? Do you have a philosophy? Number three, which we just was very very useful. Do you do your homework and now what's number four? Well number four is sometimes the toughest one for people that don't You know that aren't really well established, but it is do you have relevant and reassuring? Experience so there's an obvious catch 22 there Yeah, how do you get experience if you don't have any experience and everybody's faced that? Way back when with their first job and Everybody faces it with their first client and everybody faces it with their first client of a particular type Which is why over time the smartest copywriters and consultants tend to wind up specializing in Several to a handful of product business categories rather than What most people live with early which is Any client any product any job any place any time any price and so over time this tends to Somewhat take care of itself But it's important to understand that there's more than one path to relevant experience And so the obvious one is I've done work With this product or this business category before But there are others for example there is I've created copy that successfully has sold to the same type of customer Yes, your business is customer before so So I I understand the mentality of the customer so for example The person who buys a real high-end vacation club membership Timeshare program like destination resort cells it's about a 100 to $150,000 buy-in and Over a period of time about a half a million dollar a commitment well that customer is the same customer that buys fractional jet ownership that customer is the Barons Wall Street Journal investors business daily Subscriber Multiple subscriber because all the boring financial information available from one of those things is not enough to satisfy them They want it from three or four they tend to subscribe to Financial newsletters and advisory services and they tend to be active investors So if I've written copy that has sold to that customer That's relevant experience even though I've never touched the vacation Travel Club field so that's another way to get there it can be price so I've not had anything to do with your product or service or category of business before But I have written copy in the same price arena before well, that's relevant Whether it's low price or high price it can be processed so If for example, we're going to do lead generation lead capture online and offline multi-step follow-up and At the end of that sequence drive them To webinars and tele seminars if I've done that in business aid that has no product relationship to business be I still have relevant experience if that's the procedure we're going to use with business be So there's a number of different ways and these are just a few of them That you can present relevant and reassuring experience Even if you have not previously Done copy and created marketing for the particular product or service that we are That we're dealing with with this client Again, I think this is just giving our our attendee so much ammunition to To work with it's it's great Okay, let's let's move on number five Well, this is this is another question. That's not that fortunately is not really controlled by By by years on the job if you will There's really no benefit to old versus young or experienced versus began or whatever now when asked When enunciated by the client This is the spine chilling question How much charge how much will all this cost? Yeah? and I Say spine chilling because it's a question not a lot of people welcome And and if it's answered as is It's often fatal Because they have no frame of reference and so any amount sounds high So if if you tell The dentist that the marketing system the Advertising and follow-up mailings To bring in implant dentistry patients are good to cost him $50,000 Or you tell him it's gonna cost him 15,000 Or you tell him it's gonna cost $1,500 in most cases All three of those numbers any one of those numbers is gonna sound high to him Because he has no context for it And so it usually if you answer the fee question outright You you lose Or or at least you place yourself in a difficult environment the question has to be translated into Preferably preceded by a discussion of return on investment objectives and so You have to know or have asked about Average transaction size an example that I just use well What's an implant dentistry case? What's an average case? What's office overhead so if an average case is? $15,000 and office overhead is 50% Then an average case is gross margin gross profit is $7,500 What's the average referral rate from implant cases? Every three leads to one referral so now 33% is 7,500 I don't have a calculator here But let's call that 1500 of which 750 is gross profit So now we've got 7,500 plus 750 again. I'm adding in my head. So I think that's 82 50 So now we have first of all we have a number to work with in terms of return on investment So if the fee to put the package together is $15,000 we're really talking about two patients Right who wouldn't exist otherwise And that's a much more palatable number than is a bald-faced $15,000 Even if the fee is $50,000 right It's seven it's six point five patients That wouldn't exist otherwise, which is a whole lot more palatable number than is $50,000 Very doable Yeah, six patients very doable. Yeah, so now this doesn't work quite so well by the way with the owner of a dairy Unless of course he owns ten And so this whole numbers conversation Preferably occurs before the fee question is ever asked, but it has to occur If the fee question is now raised this conversation has to occur So that you can put fee and whatever other compensation Arrangements you may make royalties for use fees licensing fees, whatever so that you can put it into a context rather than laying it out on the floor And And so You need these numbers and you need to create what I call a possibilities a scenario Love that and and and in most cases I usually do Low medium high, you know, here's the most conservative Targets here's Middle targets here's what would be a home run and we'll all dance in the streets and I'll be a genius kind of some scenario Here's what these scenarios are here's what this looks like in return on investment further There's another part of this conversation, which I call the acquired asset conversation And so again using our dentist as an example He'll think nothing of a fifty thousand dollar price tag for a piece of equipment So the whiz bang new tooth whitening laser doohickey Or the thingamabob are that takes their pictures and shows them what their smiles gonna look like after he's done A piece of equipment is supposed to cost $50,000 and it's a physical asset You own it and you see it over there in the corner of the room and it's Bright and shiny and metal and you get that It doesn't have to be explained as an asset a Advertisement copy for the full-page newspaper ad or copy for the really small newspaper ad and the VALPAC coupon that drives to a website and copy for the eight emails that go and follow up That doesn't sit over in the corner as a big hulking piece of equipment that gets delivered in a crate And is clearly an asset, but it is just as much an asset And as a matter of fact in most cases will provide better yield than will the piece of equipment But that conversation has to occur so that the client understands that what we're about here And this is true in most cases because mostly you're not writing copy for a one-time shop Sometimes you are but if you're not if you're writing something that's going to have repetitive ongoing continuous use in our language is going to be a control of some life Then this has to be understood that we are not just Doing advertising here. We're not just creating income. We're creating an asset And so that conversation has to occur so the way we talk about money Which is preceded by the way we think about money the way we talk about money with our clients is extremely important And it is one of the things that I'll spend a fair amount of time on at the Academy at the Academy is this whole money conversation that's great because Again, I think so many people get paralyzed by it and you know by having these tools It really does take the fear away from it. It gives you power You're having a discussion with the business person about something that's going to help their business which is what they want and It just puts it on a whole different whole different level And by the way sophisticated clients of course have data To some extent in their heads and they have it accessible to them So for example the meeting I'm going to on Monday I know I know what their their CPL what their cost per order is from from their eight different main web websites and There all the print campaigns they did no nine and all the direct mail they did no nine They're a very sophisticated direct marketing company. They they had that math And they gave that math to me now the dentist May not be so sophisticated and and actually there's some virtue in being able to stump them To some extent, but that means you have to be able to guide this conversation and Get a number even if it's not a totally accurate number you have to be able to get a number or a set of numbers that you can work with and Again, if you do your homework You will at least know What some industry standards are so if you're About to go deal with the proprietor of the local bowling alley You may very well want to do a little research into the bowling alley industry and get a feel for annual revenue per lane Which is readily available from the trade association, you know, most of this is not secret and And and and have some of these numbers in your head On crib sheets if you need them so that you can effectively guide this conversation But the most important thing about all this is being able to answer the money question for the client In a way that again gives him confidence He'll trust in you and has him understanding that he's making an investment here And this is a far cry from sort of the worst end of our business where Where the client's perception is He's paying by the page or paying by the hour and that the deliverable is the CD with the copy on it and eight pages of type That you know has you like down with the secretarial service and it's not a very good place to be Well, I know we are going to be talking more about this in May And I imagine they'll be this will be part of the after-hours conversations as well, too Dan Let's let's talk a little bit about preparing for the Academy Well, one thing is of course everybody has Gotten a questionnaire. Yes who has registered and And sooner folks get those into my hands, but better Because it allows me To have an understanding of who's in the room And to tailor What it is I'm going to present to them great we have we have received some in so far they're coming here Dan We're going to collect them and then we'll send them off to you. That's great But anybody procrastinating ought not to Yeah, good thing to get done this weekend. I will tell you that just the process of doing the questionnaire is provocative and useful There's a reading list coming shortly And even though it would be helpful if you read the books on it I actually don't have that expectation So I the reading list will point to specific chapters even pages in a particular book That it will be helpful to get through before you arrive I I Look for total focus during the days that we're together. I love that. Yeah, my theory is that you can sleep at home So you don't need to really sleep here we're here to work and You ought not be Desperately waiting with bated breath for the next break that you can run out into the hall and jump on your cell phone and check your emails and all of that is a giant distraction and like frankly unless you're a bail bondsman or cardiac doctor If you can't turn that stuff off for two days You got bigger problems in your life than we're going to solve here I really encourage people to let everybody in the world know that they're going to Mars For a couple of days and there's no cell tower there They're going to be in communicato and I always point out to people that you don't see me Doing any of that on the brakes So if I can manage it you can manage it too We've got a lot to do in a short period of time. It's going to be intense. It's important to be well rested beforehand Also, if you I suggest the magic genie list now What is that? Well, that's if you found the bottle on the beach and you rubbed it and This will date me but Barbara Eden came out Or or or some other genie came out and gave you three wishes About your your business your career gave you three things that you could have and Obviously the more specific your request The better and there's jokes about that that we don't have time for What would those three things be now? It's a useful clarity exercise For everybody it's my contention that that a lot of the reasons people Don't really get what they needed or wanted from a seminar is because they played blind archery while they were there You know, they came without real clarity of What it is that was most important to get And beyond using it for your own clarity if anybody wants to share theirs by all means send those in too and if you And there's no guarantee you're going to get it or how many of them we're going to be able to do but if you have a particular Situation that you think would be Useful for you and useful for the group has a as an on-stage hot seat So well, we're going to talk about a client situation a project A business issue so maybe you're transitioning from day job to Freelancing if you if you want to if you want to have a one-on-one Discussion about that and you're willing to sort of bear your soul about it in front of everybody else then you might want to apply for one of those and Sort of a brief one-page memo about all that We'll we'll get get that done great and we'll send you instructions of where to where to send send that Okay, now we have about 10 15 minutes left for some Q&A and Alicia who's our operator is going to come on and Let you know how to get in line Ladies and gentlemen to ask a question, please press star one on your touch-tone phone You will hear the message you have entered the queue if you enter the queue accidentally you can press star one again to be removed Otherwise, I will open your lines one at a time to ask your question and ask you to state your name If you're in the queue to ask a question, please state your name and you'll hear your name repeated back into the conference You may go ahead and answer your question So if anybody would like to ask please press star one now, okay? And well while we're doing that we did get one question in from by email from Stacy Morris and When she heard the topic her response was hey Dan perfect topic One of my copy mentors always yells at me for not asking for enough money But right now my clients are small businesses who have no idea what copy costs or even what it's supposed to do How can I make copy seem more valuable than pretty designs logos and all the rest of the? I'm not sure what this word is Roco co Well We've talked a little bit about this obviously on this call And there's a lot packed into her question So let's start with the idea that we're not going to ask for money So how we think about what it is that we do? And the compensation that we get Is important and so that's it's not just a semantics exercise. It's important. We're not going to ask Anybody for money and whether enough or not enough There's we're good to present Programs and we're good to present compensation Requirements, but we're not asking Asking is one step away from begging Secondly where she said right now My clients are small businesses who have no idea what copy costs or what it's supposed to do Part of the part of the answer to this is To to attract clients and they can be small business clients Who do in fact know what copy is and what it's supposed to do and so The the sources the clients are coming from the methods you have in place So for example in 1991 Why should I go back for the 1991? My first book the ultimate sales water was published But it was self-published has how to write a million dollar on sales water four years before that and and when a client came from that book From and they were offered at that time small companies small businesses, but They had an awareness of what copy was and what it was supposed to do and the fact that they wanted it new client prospects Have always gotten material for me that they're required to read and listen to Before we have a conversation. So there's there's what client do you attract and how do you educate the client? Before you get to this stage And then how do you make the copy seem more valuable? Well first of all you you make the copy As as valuable as it really is Perception matters, but but we're not doing We're not doing trickery here. We're not making it seem more valuable than it is. We want it to be understood For it's for it's that value and So the things we were just talking about the the asset conversation the ROI conversation Is extremely important? Analogies can sometimes help educate the uneducated client so The equipment analogy for example that I used Quite frankly, I didn't just think of that for this call That analogy has been in my pocket for many many years pointing out companies that a client admires in his Category pointing out how much of their business is really all about their advertising and their marketing and the copy that drives it rather than their products or services Can help educate people So regardless of whether your client is the small local business or or or bigger Really the same principles the same strategies apply Stacey's definitely going to come out the back door of this Academy Yeah, much more adept at this and more confident about it than she is going in Here she has somebody yelling at her about Being better compensated That's a good thing and And so in that respect I'll be an echo great Okay, at least are there any questions? So you'll be fed stuff all between now and May that will help you prepare Great and Brad actually just signed up today So you haven't gotten your questionnaire yet, but it'll be going out to you tomorrow So we're we're I'm glad you got in in time for the call today Okay, we do have another question. Oh, it's another question Dan Caller and Q. Please say your name Melanie Rembrandt go ahead and ask your question Hi, Dan. I have a quick question before we end this call I was wondering if you have any final tips on how to steal the deal with the client and how long you wait for a yes Or no answer before you move on Well, those are those are two really good questions. Thank you. I snuck them both in Let me say sort of has a big umbrella to it that in any selling my contention always is that If there's a stall a delay or a problem at closing the sale Which you don't need is a better closing technique You need a better process because the close should happen automatically Now that doesn't mean you don't want some tactics in your pocket, but still generally speaking the client should be Pushing you Not you pushing them for how fast can we get started? And what's the timetable going to look like and all of that? In terms of timing That kind of depends on your process Mine Personally 90% of the time they are they're new They're with me for a day that is Diagnostic and then prescriptive And so the hiring then occurs right at the end of that day And it is usually about choosing plan a or plan B Really having a discussion of timing more than anything else but but rarely is it we're going to go home and sleep on this and talk to our wife dog and accountant and Get back to you That doesn't happen If I go further back in time When it used to happen a lot very early is when I was dumb enough to do proposals Instead of literally making the the plan part of the consultation And so that was a delay factor The other thing I'll tell you is is that the sense of supply and demand Is important here and so They have to feel like there's a limited amount of you and there's limited slots And if they mess around They're either going to have to go to the back of the line and wait Or they may not be able to get what they want within the time frame that they wanted and there's lots of ways to Imply that there's ways to demonstrate it Everything from published schedules to my own preference is to do my initial consultations in my actual work environment at my home and They see the little work stacks and the client names on them and They see the work calendar on the wall and and the message is pretty clear And so there's a lot of things to think about That precede The actual are we going to do this are we not going to do this and are we going to start now or are we going to start all later? Now having said all of that a Couple of more direct and specific answers to your question Is all revolve around a discussion now of Timing and so If the discussion is now about When they want to have results When they want to have a campaign in place Then working backwards Enables you to say Here's when we need to start and here's what has to happen next the other thing is that these conversations ideally are Assumptive not speculative So my language is all here's what's going to happen Here's what I'm going to do here's what you need to do When I prepare your Formal agreement it's going to come with your homework list and I need you to Respond to that as quickly as possible. So there's a lot of a sumptive language And There's a I have a little process. I'll show you at the academy For how I Calculate fees is transparent. I explain it and I do it in front of them So there's no delay for me between the The diagnostic and prescriptive conversation and the action plan it's seamless And and and I think when you put a delay in it you encouraged a delay Hope that's helpful I Melony Melanie with us is Melanie gone. I think she might be Melanie's gone. Okay. Well, thanks for the question Okay We are up against seven o'clock. Yes, so Dan, thank you so much and And I did want to just tell everybody how much Denise and I are looking forward to seeing you we're going to be there with you and in in Dan's Dan's Academy and We'll be in touch with you very soon with with with the next steps Yep, and probably within two weeks everybody will have my reading list great great. I'm looking forward to that Thanks, Katie. Thanks everybody. Okay, Dan. Thanks so much Bye-bye
